title: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease
section: Genetics, Aging, and Frontiers in Medicine
authors: Jonathan Cedernaes, Kathryn Moynihan Ramsey, Joseph Bass

items.subtopic: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease | BASIC EVOLUTION AND STRUCTURE OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM
items.title: Understanding the Hierarchical Structure of Mammalian Circadian Systems
items.content: Mammalian circadian systems are organized hierarchically with a master pacemaker located in the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the anterior hypothalamus. This SCN synchronizes central and peripheral clocks through various signals, including physiological rhythms like core body temperature, autonomic nervous system activity, and neuroendocrine pathways such as cortisol from the HPA axis.
keyPoints: The SCN is the master circadian pacemaker in mammals.
SCN coordinates with both extra-SCN and peripheral clocks.
Synchronization involves signals like core body temperature, autonomic nervous system activity, and neuroendocrine pathways (e.g., cortisol).
Circadian systems anticipate daily environmental changes.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease | BASIC EVOLUTION AND STRUCTURE OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM

items.subtopic: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease | BASIC EVOLUTION AND STRUCTURE OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM
items.title: Evolutionary Development of Circadian Rhythms
items.content: Circadian rhythms have evolved over billions of years, initially regulating photosynthetic processes in cyanobacteria around 3.4 billion years ago when Earth's rotation period was approximately 8 hours. These rhythms now govern a wide range of biochemical and physiological processes across all photosensitive organisms.
keyPoints: Circadian clocks evolved alongside photosynthesis for energetic regulation.
The first biological clock gene appeared in cyanobacteria around 3.4 billion years ago.
Earth's rotation period was approximately 8 hours at that time, extending to about 24 hours today.
Circadian rhythms coordinate oxygenic reactions and DNA repair processes with sunlight cycles.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease | BASIC EVOLUTION AND STRUCTURE OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR ORGANIZATION OF THE MAMMALIAN CIRCADIAN CLOCK
items.title: Understanding Circadian Rhythms: The Role of Core Clock Genes and Their Impact on Physiology
items.content: Mammalian circadian rhythms are driven by a transcription-translation feedback loop involving key transcription factors such as CLOCK/NPAS2 and BMAL1. These drive the expression of repressors PERs and CRYs, forming a cycle that repeats every 24 hours. The core clock genes not only regulate their own expression but also influence downstream clock-controlled genes (CCGs), which in turn affect various physiological processes.
keyPoints: CLOCK/NPAS2 and BMAL1 are central to the transcription-translation feedback loop driving circadian rhythms.
Deletion of Bmal1 leads to behavioral arrhythmicity, highlighting its critical role in maintaining circadian rhythms.
NPAS2 can substitute for CLOCK within pacemaker neurons, allowing rhythmicity even when Clock is absent.
~82% of protein-coding transcripts exhibit daily 24-h rhythms in primates across various tissues.
In rodents under constant conditions, ~3–16% of the transcriptome shows 24-h mRNA expression rhythms.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · MOLECULAR ORGANIZATION OF THE MAMMALIAN CIRCADIAN CLOCK

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR ORGANIZATION OF THE MAMMALIAN CIRCADIAN CLOCK
items.title: Peripheral and Central Circadian Clocks: Implications for Health and Disease
items.content: The circadian clock network extends beyond the SCN to peripheral tissues and extra-SCN neurons, indicating a widespread influence on gene expression. This extensive presence underscores the importance of circadian rhythms in regulating physiological processes across different tissues. Mutations in core clock genes can disrupt these rhythms, leading to various pathologies.
keyPoints: The molecular clock network is present in peripheral tissues and extra-SCN neurons, not just the SCN.
Mutations in clock genes are linked to impaired circadian rhythms and associated physiological disruptions.
Epigenetic mechanisms such as chromatin dynamics and histone acetylation play roles in regulating transcriptional CCG rhythms.
Posttranscriptional events like RNA polyadenylation and mRNA translation also exhibit circadian variation.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · MOLECULAR ORGANIZATION OF THE MAMMALIAN CIRCADIAN CLOCK

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC ORGANIZATION OF THE CIRCADIAN CLOCK NETWORK
items.title: Role of SCN in Circadian Regulation and Its Impact on Health
items.content: The suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) is the master circadian pacemaker, regulating various physiological processes including sleep-wake cycles, feeding, endocrine functions, energy expenditure, and metabolism. Lesioning of the SCN results in a complete loss of rhythmicity in these activities.
keyPoints: SCN identified as the master circadian pacemaker
Lesioning induces loss of rhythms in locomotor activity, drinking behavior, and hormone secretion
Regulates sleep-wake cycles through neural projections to sleep-promoting regions like VLPO
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ANATOMIC ORGANIZATION OF THE CIRCADIAN CLOCK NETWORK

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC ORGANIZATION OF THE CIRCADIAN CLOCK NETWORK
items.title: SCN's Influence on Peripheral Circadian Clocks and Hormonal Rhythms
items.content: The SCN communicates with peripheral clocks via neuronal projections and secreted factors, influencing circadian rhythms in various tissues. It controls the rhythmic release of hormones such as melatonin from the pineal gland and regulates neuroendocrine axes affecting daily variations in gonadotropin, thyrotropin, and somatotropin.
keyPoints: SCN influences peripheral clocks through neuronal projections
Regulates melatonin production via noradrenergic fibers to the pineal gland
Controls HPA axis rhythms affecting cortisol levels
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ANATOMIC ORGANIZATION OF THE CIRCADIAN CLOCK NETWORK

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC ORGANIZATION OF THE CIRCADIAN CLOCK NETWORK
items.title: Impact of External Factors on Circadian Rhythms and SCN Function
items.content: External factors such as light exposure and meal timing can influence circadian rhythms. Artificial light at night delays melatonin secretion, affecting sleep patterns. Meal timing can signal circadian time to peripheral tissues like the liver, potentially uncoupling them from the central pacemaker.
keyPoints: Artificial light at night delays melatonin secretion
Meal timing affects peripheral clocks independently of SCN
Temperature phase shifts peripheral tissue clocks but not the SCN
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ANATOMIC ORGANIZATION OF THE CIRCADIAN CLOCK NETWORK

items.subtopic: ENTRAINMENT AND MEASUREMENT OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 1)
items.title: Understanding Photoentrainment and Light's Role
items.content: Light exposure, particularly blue light (~480 nm), is the dominant zeitgeber for entraining the circadian system. It suppresses melatonin, promoting wakefulness during the day.
keyPoints: Blue light (~480 nm) suppresses melatonin, promoting daytime wakefulness.
Melanopsin system in retinal ganglion cells detects blue light and regulates circadian rhythms.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ENTRAINMENT AND MEASUREMENT OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 1)

items.subtopic: ENTRAINMENT AND MEASUREMENT OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 1)
items.title: Phase Response Curve (PRC) and Light Exposure
items.content: The phase response curve describes how light exposure affects circadian rhythms. Light before the core body temperature's minimum causes a phase delay, while after it causes a phase advance.
keyPoints: Light before CBT minimum causes phase delay; after causes phase advance.
Dim light (~100 lux) can produce half of the phase delay compared to much brighter exposure.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ENTRAINMENT AND MEASUREMENT OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 1)

items.subtopic: ENTRAINMENT AND MEASUREMENT OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 1)
items.title: Genetic Variability in Circadian Rhythms
items.content: Genetic variations, such as those in clock genes like PER3, influence individual responsiveness to light and circadian period length.
keyPoints: Endogenous human circadian clock runs ~24.2 h; mice run ~23.5 h.
PER3 gene polymorphism affects melatonin suppression by blue light.
Genetic loci contributing to chronotype variability are in the hundreds.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ENTRAINMENT AND MEASUREMENT OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 1)

items.subtopic: ENTRAINMENT AND MEASUREMENT OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 2)
items.title: Chronotype Assessment Using Questionnaires
items.content: The Horne-Östberg Morningness-Eveningness Questionnaire (MEQ) and the Munich ChronoType Questionnaire (MCTQ) are used to categorize individuals into chronotypes, which reflect their preferred sleep-wake timing. The MEQ scores range from morning-type to evening-type, while MCTQ focuses on the midpoint of sleep and assesses social jetlag.
keyPoints: MEQ categorizes individuals into five types based on waking time preferences.
MCTQ uses sleep midpoint as a circadian marker and evaluates differences between workday and free day sleep patterns.
~1% go to bed before 10:00 p.m. and ~8% after 3:00 a.m., according to MCTQ data from European populations.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ENTRAINMENT AND MEASUREMENT OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 2)

items.subtopic: ENTRAINMENT AND MEASUREMENT OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 2)
items.title: Melatonin as a Marker of Circadian Rhythm
items.content: Melatonin levels, measured through saliva or plasma, and its metabolite 6-sulphatoxymelatonin in urine, serve as markers for circadian rhythm. The dim light melatonin onset (DLMO) is used to determine if an individual's circadian phase is advanced or delayed, typically occurring ~2 hours before sleep.
keyPoints: Melatonin reflects SCN rhythmic function and can be measured in saliva, plasma, or urine as aMT6S.
DLMO occurs ~2 h before sleep onset and indicates whether the rhythm is phase advanced or delayed.
Exposure to natural light causes earlier DLMO and DLMoff compared to artificial light.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ENTRAINMENT AND MEASUREMENT OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 2)

items.subtopic: ENTRAINMENT AND MEASUREMENT OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 2)
items.title: Core Body Temperature as a Circadian Indicator
items.content: Core body temperature (CBT) is another indicator of circadian rhythm, correlating with melatonin phase. Under normal conditions from 2300 to 0700 h, CBT rises to ~37.4°C around 11 hours after reaching its minimum at 36.5°C in early morning.
keyPoints: CBT correlates well with melatonin rhythm and can be recorded wirelessly via rectal or swallowed probes.
Under normal lighting, CBT reaches ~37.2°C by 0900 h, peaks at ~37.4°C around 11 hours later, and drops to a daily low of 36.5°C at 0400 h.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ENTRAINMENT AND MEASUREMENT OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 2)

items.subtopic: PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 1)
items.title: Understanding Circadian Rhythm Sleep Disorders (CRSDs) and Their Impact
items.content: Circadian rhythm sleep disorders (CRSDs) occur when there is a mismatch between an individual's internal circadian rhythms and the external environment. This misalignment can result from environmental factors like light exposure or social activities, such as shift work or jet lag. CRSDs can also stem from genetic mutations affecting core clock genes, leading to intrinsic timing issues.
keyPoints: CRSDs arise due to misalignments between internal rhythms and external cycles.
Genetic mutations in core clock genes (e.g., CLOCK, PER3, CRY1) can cause CRSDs.
CRSDs often result in excessive sleepiness or depressed mood, impacting daily functioning.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 1)

items.subtopic: PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 1)
items.title: Delayed Sleep Phase Disorder: Characteristics and Management
items.content: Delayed Sleep Phase Disorder (DSPD) is a common CRSD characterized by significant delays in sleep onset and wake times, often seen in adolescents and young adults. It involves polymorphisms in clock genes like CLOCK, PER3, and CRY1, leading to longer circadian periods.
keyPoints: Prevalence of DSPD ranges from 0.2% to 16%, depending on definition.
Characterized by delayed sleep onset and wake times ('extreme night owls').
Effective treatments include bright-light therapy in the morning and melatonin administration in the evening.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 1)

items.subtopic: PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 1)
items.title: Advanced Sleep Phase Disorder: Characteristics and Treatment
items.content: Advanced Sleep Phase Disorder (ASPD) involves an early sleep-wake schedule, often seen in older adults or those with familial advanced sleep phase syndrome. It is associated with mutations in PER2 or CK1δ genes, leading to a shortened circadian period.
keyPoints: Prevalence of ASPD may be <1%, but underreported due to fewer societal conflicts.
Characterized by early evening bedtimes and morning awakenings ('extreme early birds').
Treatment includes bright light or blue-enriched phototherapy in the evening to delay circadian phase.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 1)

items.subtopic: PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 2)
items.title: Shift Work Sleep Disorder: Understanding Risks and Management
items.content: Shift Work Sleep Disorder (SWSD) is characterized by insomnia or excessive sleepiness due to work during typical sleeping hours. It's associated with increased risks of metabolic and cardiovascular diseases, particularly in older individuals.
keyPoints: SWSD symptoms include insomnia or excessive sleepiness.
Associated health risks: obesity, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, cancer.
Older adults are at higher risk due to age-related decline in maintaining wakefulness during typical wake periods.
Management includes optimizing the sleep environment, melatonin use, and timed bright-light therapy.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 2)

items.subtopic: PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 2)
items.title: Irregular Sleep-Wake Rhythm: Causes and Treatments
items.content: Irregular Sleep-Wake Rhythm (ISWR) results from damage to the SCN or reduced responsiveness to light/activity cues, often seen in neurologic impairments like Alzheimer's disease. It involves fragmented sleep without a clear circadian pattern.
keyPoints: Causes: SCN damage, reduced entraining signal responsiveness, decreased exposure to signals.
Characterized by multiple random sleep bouts despite normal total sleep time.
Associated with neurologic impairment, particularly in older individuals.
Treatment involves increased light exposure, improved sleep hygiene, and promoting social/physical activities.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 2)

items.subtopic: PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 2)
items.title: Non-24-h Sleep-Wake Rhythm Disorder: Diagnosis and Management
items.content: Non-24-h sleep-wake rhythm disorder, or free-running disorder, occurs when endogenous circadian rhythms are not synchronized with the external 24-hour cycle. It is common in blind individuals but can also affect sighted people.
keyPoints: Occurs due to inability to adjust circadian clock to a 24-h day.
Common in completely blind individuals; also reported in sighted individuals.
Symptoms include sleep-wake disruption, impaired alertness, mood issues, and social/work difficulties.
Diagnosis involves diurnal analysis of melatonin/cortisol rhythms and sleep diaries.
Treatment for sighted: bright-light therapy with timed melatonin. For blind: melatonin and dual MT1/MT2 receptor agonists.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 2)

items.subtopic: PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 3)
items.title: Understanding Jetlag: Mechanisms and Management
items.content: Jetlag occurs when traveling across time zones, causing misalignment between endogenous circadian rhythms and the new external light-dark cycle. The human circadian system adapts slowly to new time zones, shifting up to ~1.5 hours per day westward and ~1 hour eastward daily. Symptoms include insomnia, daytime sleepiness, and fatigue, typically resolving within a couple of days. Older individuals are more susceptible.
keyPoints: Circadian system shifts: ~1.5 h/day westward (phase delay), ~1 h/day eastward (phase advance)
Symptoms resolve in first few days
Older adults (>50) at higher risk
Therapeutic approaches include bright-light exposure and timed melatonin administration
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 3)

items.subtopic: PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 3)
items.title: Social Jetlag: Implications for Health
items.content: Social jetlag arises when there is a discrepancy between social and biological time, often affecting individuals with a late chronotype. It can be quantified using tools like the MCTQ, revealing that many Europeans experience 2 or more hours of social jetlag. Chronic social jetlag is linked to increased risks of obesity, metabolic syndrome, higher alcohol consumption, smoking, and poorer academic performance.
keyPoints: Social jetlag estimated with questionnaires (e.g., MCTQ)
~2+ hours of social jetlag common in European population
Associated with obesity, metabolic syndrome, increased alcohol/smoking, poor academic performance
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 3)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)
items.title: Circadian Regulation of Cortisol and Its Impact on Physiology
items.content: The circadian clock regulates cortisol rhythms through the HPA axis, influencing various physiological processes. Cortisol levels peak in the morning to prepare for daytime activities and food intake. Light exposure modulates both the cortisol awakening response (CAR) and daytime cortisol levels.
keyPoints: Cortisol release increases toward the morning.
The CAR is blunted by acute overnight wakefulness.
Light exposure affects CAR and daytime cortisol levels.
Glucocorticoid receptors are present in both CNS and peripheral tissues.
Dexamethasone synchronizes circadian rhythms of cells.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)
items.title: Role of Sleep and Light Exposure on Hormonal Rhythms
items.content: Sleep influences hormonal rhythms, with slow-wave sleep being crucial for growth hormone secretion. Melatonin amplitude is affected by sleep deprivation, which can be exacerbated by artificial light exposure at night.
keyPoints: GH secretion depends on slow-wave sleep.
Sleep deprivation increases melatonin levels.
Artificial nighttime light exposure affects melatonin.
Individual sensitivity to light varies significantly.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)
items.title: Peripheral Hormones and Their Feedback on Central Circadian Clocks
items.content: Peripherally produced hormones like cortisol, thyroid hormones, leptin, and ghrelin exhibit circadian rhythms and can feedback to central clocks. These hormones influence energy homeostasis and food intake through their rhythmic signaling.
keyPoints: Cortisol and thyroid hormones regulate their own synthesis via feedback.
Leptin is released from white adipose tissue; ghrelin from the stomach.
Ghrelin peaks relate to anticipated meal timing.
Disruption in circadian rhythms affects leptin and ghrelin levels.
Hormonal responses are gated by the molecular clock.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)
items.title: Circadian Control of Glucose Homeostasis and Its Implications
items.content: The circadian system plays a crucial role in regulating glucose homeostasis through variations in insulin sensitivity, pancreatic $\beta$-cell function, and gene expression rhythms. Disruption of these processes is linked to metabolic diseases like type 2 diabetes.
keyPoints: Oral glucose tolerance varies across the day, being lower in the evening compared to the morning due to circadian control of insulin sensitivity and secretion.
$\beta$-cell function and gene expression rhythms are altered in patients with type 2 diabetes, indicating a hereditary role for the circadian clock in metabolic disease pathogenesis.
Ablation studies in mice reveal that both central and peripheral clocks regulate energy homeostasis, affecting insulin secretion and glucose metabolism.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)
items.title: Impact of Peripheral Clocks on Metabolic Regulation
items.content: Peripheral tissue-specific clocks are essential for the circadian regulation of energy utilization, influencing insulin sensitivity and glucose disposal. Genetic modifications in these clocks can lead to metabolic dysregulation.
keyPoints: Tissue-specific clocks regulate insulin sensitivity and glucose metabolism, with peak activity in the morning and decline toward evening.
Liver-specific Bmal1 ablation affects gluconeogenesis and glycogen synthesis according to sleep/wake cycles.
Muscle-specific Bmal1 deficiency results in reduced glucose tolerance due to decreased GLUT4 levels.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)
items.title: Time-Restricted Eating and Metabolic Health
items.content: Time-restricted eating (TRE) aligns food intake with circadian rhythms, improving metabolic homeostasis and promoting weight loss. It modulates transcriptional rhythms and may enhance the regulation of sleep and hunger.
keyPoints: TRE can improve metabolic health by reducing calorie intake without active counting, leading to weight loss.
Aligning feeding times with environmental zeitgebers enhances metabolic rhythms and reduces adverse effects from mistimed eating.
Consuming a greater proportion of calories earlier in the day may confer metabolic advantages, including increased diet-induced thermogenesis.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 3)
items.title: Impact of Circadian Misalignment on Metabolic Health
items.content: Circadian misalignment, such as that caused by shift work or social jetlag, disrupts circadian clocks across tissues and leads to metabolic disturbances. This includes reduced insulin sensitivity, impaired glucose regulation due to inadequate $\beta$-cell compensation, and a decline in resting metabolic rate.
keyPoints: Circadian misalignment reduces insulin sensitivity in both pre- and postprandial states.
Insulin secretion fails to suppress glucose levels under prolonged circadian misalignment.
Resting metabolic rate declines significantly during both awake and sleeping states.
Shift work increases the risk of obesity, type 2 diabetes, and metabolic syndrome.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 3)
items.title: Genetic Influences on Circadian Rhythms and Metabolic Health
items.content: Certain genetic polymorphisms in clock genes such as BMAL1, CLOCK, NPAS2, and MTNR1B are associated with metabolic disorders. These variants can influence insulin sensitivity, waist circumference, blood glucose levels, and risk of hypertension.
keyPoints: BMAL1 polymorphism increases the risk of type 2 diabetes.
CLOCK gene variants interact with diet to affect insulin sensitivity.
Minor allele of CLOCK variant linked to increased waist circumference in high saturated fat intake individuals.
NPAS2 and BMAL1 variants associated with a greater risk of hypertension.
MTNR1B gene variants lead to elevated fasting blood glucose levels and reduced insulin secretion.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 3)
items.title: Role of Social Jetlag in Metabolic Disruption
items.content: Social jetlag, characterized by shifts between work/nonfree days and free days, is linked to metabolic disruptions such as obesity and type 2 diabetes. This results from perturbed rhythms of energy expenditure and circadian hunger drive, often exacerbated by conditions like night-eating syndrome.
keyPoints: Social jetlag increases the risk of obesity and type 2 diabetes.
Shifted feeding rhythms in relation to sleep-wake cycles contribute to metabolic disruptions.
Night-eating syndrome is associated with delayed internal clock timing and obesity.
Later chronotypes linked to type 2 diabetes and impaired glycemic control, e.g., hemoglobin $A_{1c}$ values exceeding 7%.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 4)
items.title: Impact of Shift Work on Cognitive Health
items.content: Shift work, especially with inadequate recovery periods between shifts, is associated with decreased gray matter in brain regions involved in memory and executive functions. This can lead to signs of accelerated cognitive aging. However, these effects may be reversible after cessation of shift work for 5 or more years.
keyPoints: Decreased gray matter in memory and executive function areas due to shift work
Accelerated cognitive aging observed in long-term rotating shift workers
Potential reversibility of cognitive decline after stopping shift work for over 5 years
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 4)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 4)
items.title: Circadian Rhythms and Alzheimer's Disease Pathogenesis
items.content: Disrupted circadian rhythms contribute to the pathogenesis of Alzheimer's disease. Amyloid beta levels, which fluctuate naturally with circadian cycles, show dampened rhythms in AD-prone individuals. The glymphatic system, active during sleep, plays a role in clearing amyloid beta and other neurotoxins.
keyPoints: Amyloid beta exhibits circadian fluctuations, peaking during the active period
Dampened daily rhythms of amyloid beta in AD-prone individuals
Glymphatic system facilitates toxin removal during sleep
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 4)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 4)
items.title: Bidirectional Relationship Between Circadian Rhythms and Neuropsychiatric Conditions
items.content: Shift work is linked to an increased risk of depression, particularly in women. Disrupted sleep and circadian rhythms are central features of depression and other neuropsychiatric conditions. Genetic variations in clock genes also play a role in mood disorders.
keyPoints: Increased risk of depression associated with shift work
Disruption of sleep and circadian rhythms is key in depression
Genetic variants in clock genes linked to mood disorders
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 4)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 5)
items.title: Impact of Sleep Deprivation and Circadian Misalignment on Cognitive Function
items.content: Sleep deprivation significantly reduces alertness, impairs decision-making, and increases accident risk. This impairment is comparable to mild alcohol intoxication after 18-24 hours of wakefulness. The situation worsens with circadian misalignment, such as in shift work, leading to a drastic drop in reaction speed.
keyPoints: Sleep deprivation reduces alertness and decision-making similar to mild alcohol intoxication after 18–24 h of continuous wakefulness.
Cognitive abilities suffer more when sleep restriction is combined with circadian misalignment (e.g., shift work).
A study showed a ~43-h long day protocol with reduced sleep (~5.6 h in 24 h) led to reaction speed dropping almost by an order of magnitude at the nadir of the circadian period.
Serious medical errors increased by nearly 36% among resident interns working >24-hour shifts compared to those on ≤16-hour shifts.
Errors resulting in patient death were three times more likely with extended hours (>24 h) than shorter shifts (≤16 h).
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 5)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 5)
items.title: Circadian Influence on Gastrointestinal and Microbiota Function
items.content: The gastrointestinal tract's physiological aspects, including gastric emptying, colonic motility, bile acid secretion, and nutrient transporter expression, exhibit circadian rhythms. Disruption of these rhythms through sleep fragmentation or shift work can increase intestinal permeability and alter gut microbiota composition.
keyPoints: Gastrointestinal functions like gastric emptying and colonic motility are greater during the active phase.
Circadian rhythmicity affects bile acid secretion, nutrient uptake transporters (e.g., SGLT1), and intestinal wall permeability.
Chronic sleep fragmentation increases intestinal permeability, potentially allowing inflammatory molecules to enter systemic circulation.
Gut microbiota composition and function are influenced by circadian rhythms and can be disrupted by jetlag or shift work.
Circadian disruption may increase the risk of inflammatory bowel disease and colon cancer in shift workers.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 5)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 5)
items.title: Role of Circadian Rhythms in Cardiovascular Health
items.content: Circadian rhythms significantly influence cardiovascular health, with a higher incidence of myocardial infarction and sudden cardiac death occurring in the morning. Blood pressure peaks around 2100 h and dips during sleep, which is crucial for cardiovascular prognosis.
keyPoints: Higher incidence of myocardial infarction occurs in the morning hours.
Blood pressure typically peaks at 2100 h and decreases during sleep.
A circadian nighttime dip in BP (3–6 mmHg SBP, 2–3 mmHg DBP) is associated with cardiovascular health.
BP dipping <10% or >20% during normal sleep correlates with worse cardiovascular prognosis and dementia risk.
Disrupted nighttime BP dipping is linked to increased cardiovascular disease risk in conditions like insomnia and narcolepsy.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 5)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 6)
items.title: Circadian Influence on Cardiovascular Risk
items.content: The circadian system affects cardiovascular health by influencing heart rate and blood pressure, with increased risks in the morning. Misalignment can elevate these parameters, contributing to higher cardiovascular disease risk.
keyPoints: Heart rate decreases during sleep; mistimed sleep increases it.
Cardiac procedures scheduled for afternoon may have better outcomes due to circadian timing.
Morning poses a greater risk of cardiovascular events.
Shift work is linked with dyslipidemia, hypertension, and coronary heart disease risks.
Circadian misalignment elevates 24-hour blood pressure, especially during sleep.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 6)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 6)
items.title: Circadian Disruption's Role in Cancer
items.content: Disruption of circadian rhythms is associated with increased cancer risk and poorer prognosis. Mechanisms include altered cell cycle regulation, telomere shortening, and impaired apoptosis.
keyPoints: Shift work involving circadian disruption is likely carcinogenic (IARC classification).
Increased risks for colon and breast cancers are linked to shift work.
Telomere shortening observed in shift workers may contribute to cancer risk.
Time-restricted feeding can optimize circadian processes, reducing cancer risk.
Genetic studies show clock disruption promotes lung tumorigenesis and lymphoma.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 6)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 6)
items.title: Impact of Circadian Misalignment on Immune Function
items.content: Circadian misalignment and sleep restriction impair immune cell function, altering cytokine rhythms and reducing immunosurveillance efficiency. This can lead to increased inflammatory markers and reduced vaccine efficacy.
keyPoints: Misalignment alters immune cell levels and reactive radical production.
Disruption affects pathogen-induced cytotoxic T-cell response timing.
Increased inflammatory markers (e.g., C-reactive protein, TNF-α) are noted with circadian misalignment.
Circadian disruption reduces T-cell antigen response; vaccination in the morning is more effective.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 6)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 7)
items.title: Impact of Aging on Circadian Rhythms and Health
items.content: Aging leads to significant changes in circadian rhythms, including reduced amplitude and phase advances. These alterations are linked with increased risks for various health conditions.
keyPoints: Aging is associated with a decline in the robustness of intrinsic rhythmic processes at behavioral, physiologic, and molecular levels.
Old rodents are 20 times less sensitive to light entrainment compared to young animals.
Dampened rest-activity amplitude correlates with increased risk for dementia, CVD, cancer, and all-cause mortality.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 7)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 7)
items.title: Molecular Changes in Aging Circadian Clocks
items.content: Aging affects the expression of core clock genes like Clock and Bmal1, impacting circadian rhythms at a molecular level.
keyPoints: Decreased expression and altered diurnal profiles of core clock genes such as Clock and Bmal1 are observed in aging.
Acute induction of Per1 in response to light is reduced in aged mice SCN.
Bmal1 deficiency leads to premature accumulation of reactive oxygen species and age-related pathologies.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 7)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 7)
items.title: Potential Interventions for Circadian Rhythm Restoration in Aging
items.content: Behavioral and pharmacologic interventions can potentially restore circadian rhythms and delay aging-related declines.
keyPoints: Restoration of NAD⁺ levels with nicotinamide riboside (NR) supplementation restores metabolic and stress response pathways.
Transplantation of the SCN from a young rat into an old rat rescues locomotor activity and CRH rhythms.
Physical activity or targeted therapeutics may ameliorate circadian deterioration in aged humans.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 7)

items.subtopic: CHRONOTHERAPY AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS (part 1)
items.title: Optimal Timing of Statin Administration
items.content: Statins are more effective when administered in the evening due to the peak in endogenous cholesterol synthesis during early morning hours.
keyPoints: Endogenous cholesterol synthesis peaks in the early morning.
Evening administration of statins aligns with this peak, optimizing LDL-C reduction.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · CHRONOTHERAPY AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CHRONOTHERAPY AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS (part 1)
items.title: Timing of ACE Inhibitors for Blood Pressure Control
items.content: ACE inhibitors are most effective at night to normalize blood pressure rhythms and restore the nighttime dip in BP.
keyPoints: Blood pressure is lowest during sleep.
Nighttime administration of ACE inhibitors helps restore the natural dip in BP.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · CHRONOTHERAPY AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CHRONOTHERAPY AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS (part 1)
items.title: Chronotherapy in Cancer Treatment
items.content: Administering cancer treatments at specific times can enhance effectiveness and reduce toxicity.
keyPoints: 5-fluorouracil is more effective for colorectal cancer when given at night.
Doxorubicin for ovarian cancer is less toxic if administered early in the morning.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · CHRONOTHERAPY AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CHRONOTHERAPY AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS (part 2)
items.title: Pharmacologic Control of Circadian Rhythms in Metabolic Disorders
items.content: Emerging compounds from drug discovery screens can modulate circadian clock components, offering potential treatments for disorders with a circadian component. These include CRY stabilizers and inhibitors targeting CKI $ \delta $, CKI $ \epsilon $, and GSK-3.
keyPoints: Novel compounds impact circadian clock components by either shortening or lengthening the period.
Compounds identified: CRY stabilizers, inhibitors of CKI $ \delta $, CKI $ \epsilon $, and GSK-3.
Potential applications in treating circadian disorders and metabolic disturbances with a circadian component such as type 2 diabetes and metabolic syndrome.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · CHRONOTHERAPY AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS (part 2)

items.subtopic: CHRONOTHERAPY AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS (part 2)
items.title: Impact of Circadian Biology on Drug Timing and Metabolic Disorders
items.content: Understanding circadian control over biological functions can illuminate the pathogenesis of metabolic disorders with a circadian component, influencing optimal drug delivery timing for improved patient care.
keyPoints: Circadian processes modulate physiology and are linked to metabolic disorders like type 2 diabetes and metabolic syndrome.
Insights into circadian control mechanisms can improve understanding of disease pathogenesis.
Timing of drug delivery based on circadian biology may enhance treatment efficacy for patients with these conditions.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · CHRONOTHERAPY AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS (part 2)

items.explanation: The suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the anterior hypothalamus acts as the 'master' circadian pacemaker in mammals.

options: It reacts to daily changes in the external environment.
It enables organisms to anticipate daily environmental changes.
items.explanation: A defining property of the circadian clock system is that it enables organisms to anticipate, rather than simply react to, daily changes in the external environment.

items.subtopic: PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 1)

options: Dark-room therapy in the morning and bright-light therapy in the evening
Bright-light therapy after waking in the morning and melatonin administration in the evening
Avoiding light exposure during the day

items.explanation: Mutations in the CLOCK gene can lead to a longer circadian period and are associated with Delayed Sleep Phase Disorder.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 1)

Chronic insomnia unrelated to work schedule

items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · PRIMARY PATHOLOGIES OF THE CIRCADIAN SYSTEM (part 3)

items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

Whole-body Clock mutant mice develop obesity without hyperinsulinemia, indicating $\beta$-cell failure.
Clock gene ablation has no significant effect on metabolic homeostasis in mice.

It disrupts mitochondrial oxidative metabolism in the wake/feeding period.

It ameliorates metabolic phenotypes from high-fat diets by promoting beta oxidation of fatty acids.

Mice fed a high-fat diet exclusively during their active dark period gain more weight.
There is no difference in weight gain between feeding during the light or dark periods.

items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 4)
items.question: Which brain regions are most affected by decreased gray matter in shift workers?
options: Regions involved in memory and executive functions
items.explanation: Shift workers have decreased gray matter in brain regions involved in memory and executive functions.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 4)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 4)
items.question: What is the role of the glymphatic system in relation to amyloid beta ($A\beta$) clearance?
options: It removes neurotoxic substances like $A\beta$ during nighttime sleep.
It has no effect on $A\beta$ levels.
It enhances $A\beta$ accumulation in the brain.

items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 4)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 4)
items.question: What is a common circadian rhythm disturbance observed in Alzheimer's disease patients?
options: Sundowning, with agitation in the afternoon or evening
items.explanation: 'Sundowning' is a phenomenon where Alzheimer's patients become more agitated and exhibit delirium-like symptoms in the afternoon or evening.

options: Decreasing outdoor activity during the day
Increasing daytime light exposure and including melatonin supplementation
items.explanation: Interventions that increase daytime light exposure and include melatonin supplementation ameliorate symptoms of Alzheimer's disease.

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 5)
items.question: Which factor exacerbates the decline in cognitive abilities when combined with sleep restriction?

items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 5)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 5)
items.question: What was the observed effect on reaction speed during a forced desynchrony protocol coupled with sleep loss?
options: Reaction speed increased by an order of magnitude
No significant change in reaction speed
Reaction speed dropped almost by an order of magnitude
items.explanation: Subjects' reaction speed dropped almost by an order of magnitude when tested at the nadir of their circadian period.

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 5)
items.question: What is a potential consequence of increased intestinal permeability due to chronic sleep fragmentation?

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 5)
items.question: What is associated with a greater incidence of myocardial infarction?
items.explanation: There is a greater incidence of myocardial infarction in the morning hours.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 5)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 6)
items.question: Which of the following is a potential consequence of circadian misalignment on cardiovascular health?
items.explanation: Circadian misalignment elevates 24-h BP, particularly during sleep, and is associated with increased cardiovascular risks.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 6)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 6)
items.question: What has been observed in shift workers that may contribute to cancer risk?
items.explanation: Shift work is associated with telomere shortening, which may destabilize the genome and increase cancer risk.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 6)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 6)
items.question: Which of the following findings supports a link between circadian disruption and immune function?
options: Enhanced pathogen-induced effector response at night
Elevated levels of inflammatory markers with circadian misalignment
items.explanation: Circadian misalignment increases inflammatory markers and impairs the immune system's ability to mount an appropriate response during the active period.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 6)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 6)
items.question: What is a potential benefit of time-restricted feeding in relation to cancer risk?
options: Increases food intake during the night
Optimizes circadian processes and reduces carcinogenic cell damage
items.explanation: Time-restricted feeding optimizes circadian processes, potentially reducing the risk of carcinogenic cell damage.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 6)

items.question: What is a common behavioral change associated with aging related to circadian rhythms?
items.explanation: Aging leads to reduced and fragmented sleep, which is a common behavioral change associated with circadian rhythm instability.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 7)

items.subtopic: ROLE OF THE CLOCK SYSTEM IN PHYSIOLOGY (part 7)

items.assertion: Time of day is an important consideration for the diagnosis and treatment of metabolic disorders such as type 2 diabetes.
items.reason: Diabetes patients may experience the dawn phenomenon or Somogyi effect due to circadian rhythms affecting insulin secretion and glucose tolerance.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why time of day is important.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · CHRONOTHERAPY AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CHRONOTHERAPY AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS (part 1)
items.assertion: Continuous measurements of 24-h glucose levels indicate that daytime blood glucose levels more accurately predict glucoregulatory parameters.
items.reason: Nighttime blood glucose levels are found to be more predictive of glucoregulatory parameters than daytime levels.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; nighttime glucose levels, not daytime, more accurately predict glucoregulatory parameters.
items.reference: The Role of Circadian Biology in Health and Disease · CHRONOTHERAPY AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CHRONOTHERAPY AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS (part 2)
items.assertion: Novel compounds affecting circadian clocks can be used in treating circadian and metabolic disorders.
items.reason: These compounds include CRY stabilizers and various inhibitors of CKI $ \delta $, CKI $ \epsilon $, and GSK-3.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
